The A–Z of Japan: A Crash Course for Smart Travellers (part 2)
[
![Japan Unravelled: Insider](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!T2-D!,w_40,h_40,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb9313a9d-955e-4893-8191-ef8d7e86d02d_1280x1280.png)
](https://japanunravelled.substack.com/)
# [Japan Unravelled: Insider](https://japanunravelled.substack.com/)
SubscribeSign in
# The A–Z of Japan: A Crash Course for Smart Travellers (part 2)
### 26 essential words and concepts every traveller can expect to encounter - and exactly what they mean
[
![Andrew's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PsDy!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fc89618fc-02cc-4dac-9679-a7330f9b16d1_1595x1573.jpeg)
](https://substack.com/@japanunravelled)
[Andrew](https://substack.com/@japanunravelled)
Aug 01, 2025
24
2
Share
*In case you missed it, the first half of this A-Z [was covered in part 1](https://japanunravelled.substack.com/p/the-az-of-japan-a-crash-course-for).*
Today I’m continuing with more Japanese words and concepts (as well as a few extras) you’ll come across while planning a trip. Too much jargon adds extra stress, especially for first-time visitors to Japan, so read on to get up to speed.
**N is for Nihongo**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!T-Uc!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3a1e6f22-919c-42d5-808e-e06b4540ed42_640x384.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!T-Uc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/3a1e6f22-919c-42d5-808e-e06b4540ed42_640x384.jpeg)
*Nihongo *means the Japanese language. You might be asked *nihongo o hana-saymaskcar? (can you speak Japanese?)* by somewhat optimistic locals.
But do you need to learn lots of Japanese before you visit?
Probably not.
I’ve heard of many travellers learning Japanese on apps like duolingo before their trips. To me, this is overkill, by a long way.
So can you use English everywhere?
Not really. But don’t worry.
I find that travellers most commonly get into communication trouble when they haven’t done their homework. You can cut down on stress and the need to ask questions by just checking things in advance.
Even when websites are only available in Japanese, autotranslate can fix that, and you can quickly find out what you need to know.
Beyond doing some research, I strongly recommend just [learning the basics that I cover in this video](https://youtu.be/BhYq50TmqTI). When that isn’t enough, apps like [VoiceTra](https://voicetra.nict.go.jp/), [Deepl](https://www.deepl.com/en/translator), or Google Translate will do.
**O is for Onsen**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WQQ_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fa979f724-9301-426f-908c-a2ddfc9048e1_640x427.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WQQ_!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/a979f724-9301-426f-908c-a2ddfc9048e1_640x427.jpeg)
*Onsen* means hot spring - but when we talk about *onsen*, we’re usually referring to the resort towns that inevitably spring up in the area (sorry), and specifically the baths filled with the naturally-heated water.
Baths tend to be indoors and sex-segregated, however in some places you’ll find outdoor options. Oh, and you’re going to need to be 100% naked for this experience - no bathing suits allowed. (Some places have private *onsen* though, which you may prefer.)
Beware if you have tattoos - thanks to these being linked to the mafia, you’ll usually be denied entry. If you’re in this category, check ahead, because fortunately, [tatoo-friendly ](https://japanese-onsen.com/ryokans-tattoo-friendly-onsen/?_ryokan_types=tattoos-allowed)*[onsen](https://japanese-onsen.com/ryokans-tattoo-friendly-onsen/?_ryokan_types=tattoos-allowed) do* exist.
My top 3 *onsen* destinations are Kusatsu, Kurokawa, and Beppu. But I’m not an expert.
[Share](https://japanunravelled.substack.com/p/the-az-of-japan-a-crash-course-for-fda?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&action=share)
**P is for Purikura**
*Lost In Translation* got it right: a visit to a video games arcade (known as a “game centre”) is one of the fastest ways of escaping reality and diving head first into full-on crazy Japan.
But just a few steps away from that drumming game and you’ll find the *purikura* machines.
Short for “print club”, these machines are extremely popular with teenage girls.
Unlike those boring passport photo booths, *purikura* machines allow you to decorate your snaps with quirky designs like fancy glasses, cat ears, sparkling stars - you name it, the machines have it.
When you’re done decorating (and there’s a time limit, because otherwise people would never go home) the machine will print out your creations, which you can cut and stick wherever you like, because, yes, these are photo stickers you’re getting!
**Q is for Ta Q Bin**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TgD2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe84a5628-7cb9-4ed0-847d-aa4bb511e8f3_640x360.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TgD2!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/e84a5628-7cb9-4ed0-847d-aa4bb511e8f3_640x360.jpeg)
*Takkyubin* is the name of a delivery service run by a company called Yamato. It’s a brand name that’s taken over from the generic word, simply because of how popular Yamato’s service has become.
Takkyubin is well-known as a way of sending parcels and other items anywhere in Japan for next-day delivery. There are, of course, some exceptions to that - like when the destination is in Okinawa or Hokkaido, or when weather conditions (often typhoons) or earthquakes make delivery slightly trickier than usual.
Takkyubin is the most-commonly used service for luggage forwarding by visitors. Hotels commonly help with filling in the form (which is easy) when you want to send some cases on to your next place. If they don’t, you can always take your luggage to the nearest convenience store and send from there.
Before you send luggage, it’s essential to check with the destination hotel that they accept forwarded cases. Sometimes, you’ll find it’s not possible. If that happens, don’t worry. Just search for the nearest Yamato depot to your hotel on Google Maps, and send the cases there. Thankfully, Yamato depots are everywhere.
Yes, this service does cost money, but I think you’ll find that the price is extremely reasonable, because it saves you from an awful lot of hassle. I hate taking cases on trains - and when I can pay a little bit to get treated like a VIP, I pay it. Try it - and enjoy!
Subscribe
**R is for Ryokan**
*Ryokan* are old-style Japanese inns with traditional, legendary hospitality. Rooms at *ryokan* usually have *tatami* flooring, *futon *for sleeping, *yukata *instead of pajamas, a communal bath, and set times for breakfast and dinner. It’s worth spending at least one night in a *ryokan* for the experience.
**S is for Shinkansen**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mALb!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F26a9d49b-06bf-44c3-a3c8-c3ea7e3184ba_640x480.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!mALb!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/26a9d49b-06bf-44c3-a3c8-c3ea7e3184ba_640x480.jpeg)
This is the Japanese word for bullet train, but it literally means “new trunk line”. This is because the first s*hinkansen* line between Tokyo and Osaka was designed as a high-speed upgrade to the main (trunk) Tokaido Line which was using (and still uses) slower, conventional trains.
Riding the *shinkansen* is almost always an absolute pleasure. Trains run to time, with average delays measured in seconds, rather than minutes. Seats have plenty of legroom, and you can stow cases overhead. It’s easy to reserve a seat at ticket machines and offices, and you can also buy tickets online.
Today there are lots of *shinkansen* lines spread across the country, meaning you can get to many places relatively quickly - although covering large distances can get expensive. Look into one of the passes offered by Japan Railways (JR) if you want to cut costs.
**T is for Tatami**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!HryR!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F000919aa-43e2-409e-8f0d-f65c9e8e2a77_640x427.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!HryR!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/000919aa-43e2-409e-8f0d-f65c9e8e2a77_640x427.jpeg)
These are the yellowy-brown woven floor mats you’ll see everywhere inside traditional Japanese houses, many temple buildings, and *ryokan*. Walking on *tatami* with your shoes on is a big no-no, and will draw gasps from any Japanese nearby.
You have been warned.
**U is for Ume**
*Ume* means plum, and these trees blossom from late January to early March.
Perfect if you want to experience something like *sakura*, just without the crowdsurfing.
Thanks for reading Japan Unravelled: Insider! This post is public so feel free to share it.
[Share](https://japanunravelled.substack.com/p/the-az-of-japan-a-crash-course-for-fda?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&action=share)
**V is for Vending Machines**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!B-5i!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F21a1541a-63b4-480f-a10e-1beb6b3c018b_640x427.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!B-5i!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/21a1541a-63b4-480f-a10e-1beb6b3c018b_640x427.jpeg)
Japanese doesn’t really have a pure “V” sound (it’s more a mashup of “V” and “B”) so I’m cheating on this letter.
But anyway, the vending machines are everywhere in Japan, to the extent that it’s quite difficult to get properly thirsty.
**W is for Washoku**
Sometimes at hotels or *ryokan*, you’ll be given a choice of breakfast style. It’s good to know in advance so you don’t need to think too much. *Washoku* means Japanese style, and you can expect a bowl of rice, some pickles, miso soup, some cooked fish, and probably some natto if the hotel is particularly unkind.
*Yoshoku* on the other hand means “western-style”, with “style” being the most important bit of that word. This means you’ll get Japan’s idea of what a western breakfast looks like, with something like scrambled eggs, sausage, salad, coffee, and a very thick slice of bread with butter and jam.
**X is for X**
Not a word as such, but if someone makes an X by crossing their arms, stop whatever it is that you’re doing.
This gesture means “expressly forbidden”.
Japan Unravelled: Insider is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.
Subscribe
**Y is for Yukata, Kimono and Jinbei**
[
![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!InQ0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5322a12d-a873-4f4d-9695-259c7ef7803e_640x360.jpeg)
](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!InQ0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https://substack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com/public/images/5322a12d-a873-4f4d-9695-259c7ef7803e_640x360.jpeg)
Getting kitted out in traditional dress is quite a popular thing for visitors to do.
*Yukata* are lightweight robes made from cotton or linen, designed to be worn in the hot summer months.
*Kimono* are generally made from silk and are heavier, and worn in seasons when you’re not going to dehydrate to death in 60 seconds flat. You may have the idea that kimono are only worn by women, but men can wear them too (just with different designs of course).
Lastly, *jinbei* are are like a more informal version of *yukata* - lightweight top and bottom sets, worn as pajamas in *ryokan*, capsule hotels, and at summer fireworks festivals.
**Z is for Zen**
This is the Japanese pronunciation for a Chinese word Ch’an that means “meditation”. Zen gardens, like the one at Ryōan-ji in Kyoto, are intended to help people meditate.
Rocks, gravel, water, or sand are used to symbolise aspects of nature, such as mountains or rivers in the surrounding area.
So although the garden may not look it, each component has been positioned with great care and thought.
Subscribe
### What video should I make next?
I’d like to hear your thoughts. Drop them in the comments, or send me a friendly message using my contact form.
[Contact form](https://japanunravelled.com/contact/)
### Videos you might have missed
I was planning to make only one new video of Kyoto’s hidden gems. But I had so many, I split it into two. Enjoy!
[![Embedded YouTube video](https://img.youtube.com/vi/QipNMjZQliA/0.jpg)](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=QipNMjZQliA)

### Trip Essentials
Get ready to travel and support my work at the same time!
**Work with me:** [Japan Plan Support](https://japanunravelled.com/get-my-help/) (Paid Consulting)
**My e-book:** [Japan Out There: Kansai Edition](https://japanunravelled.com/JOT-K)
**Basics:** [eSIM](https://japanunravelled.com/e-sim) / [Sim Card](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/sim-card) / [Pocket Wifi](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/pocket-wifi) / [VPN](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/vpn) / [Travel Insurance](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/travel-insurance) / [Wise Prepaid Card](https://wise.com/invite/dic/andrewt103)
**Transport: **[JR Pass](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/Japan-Rail-Pass) / [Regional Passes](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/Regional-Passes) / [Shinkansen Tickets](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/Shinkansen-tickets) / [Car rental](https://japanunravelled.com/rec/car-rental)
**Youtube:** [Get ready for Japan playlist](https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLtS_rSTB2Xkk4pNSBc-OxUlrI2xcJBecU)
#### That’s all for this month! See ya!
#### Andrew
\*There are some affiliate links in this email: I will earn a small commission if you buy something, and it costs you nothing. Cheers!

](https://substack.com/profile/42474699-tirion?utm_source=comment)
[Tirion](https://substack.com/profile/42474699-tirion?utm_source=substack-feed-item)
[Aug 2](https://japanunravelled.substack.com/p/the-az-of-japan-a-crash-course-for-fda/comment/141324948)
Great post, Andrew - thank you!
With 37 million tourists visiting Japan in 2024 and more than 3 million foreigners living in Japan, I think Japan could try a lot harder with language for both groups. Relatively few foreigners come to Japan able to speak good Japanese. Even after living here for decades, foreigners living in Japan are probably still going to be more comfortable in their native language.
Yes, of course it makes sense to learn Japanese; but it would be kind and considerate if websites and product information were not provided in Japanese only in Japan. In overseas markets, Japanese corporations furnish multi-lingual websites and product literature, user manuals, etc. It would be good manners to extend the same courtesy to foreigners living in Japan.
Heck, I have often been disappointed by the absence of English signage and information even at major tourist sites. Given Japan's precarious demographic and financial position, it seems rather short-sighted - verging on denial - for Japan to display such an, er, "insular" mentality.
